question: A 1-year-old girl presents with seizures and undergoes brain MRI as part of the evaluation. Based on the imaging, which of the following clinical features would be most likely for this patient?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the coronal brain MRI. The cerebral hemispheres show a markedly simplified gyral pattern with very shallow or absent sulci and a thickened-appearing cortex bilaterally. The appearance is symmetric between the two hemispheres, and there is relative enlargement of the lateral ventricles. There is no focal destructive lesion or strictly unilateral abnormality visible.

Next, I relate these imaging findings to the clinical question, which asks which clinical feature is most likely for this 1-year-old with seizures. The image demonstrates a diffuse, bilateral cortical malformation (a smooth/thick cortex) rather than a focal cortical lesion.

Then, I consider how each option aligns with that pattern. Severe global developmental delay (A) is consistent with a widespread cortical malformation affecting both hemispheres because such diffuse abnormalities typically impair overall brain development and function. Rapid head growth/macrocephaly (B) is not suggested by the image—the ventricles are enlarged but head size cannot be inferred from this slice and there is no focal mass to explain progressive macrocephaly. Isolated behavioral regression only (C) would imply relatively preserved global development aside from behavior, which does not match the extensive bilateral cortical abnormality seen here. Focal unilateral hemiparesis (D) would be expected with a focal, asymmetric lesion, but the MRI shows symmetric, diffuse cortical involvement rather than a unilateral lesion.

Therefore, the imaging pattern of diffuse bilateral cortical malformation best supports severe global developmental delay as the most likely associated clinical feature.
caption: Coronal T2WI of the brain of 1-year-old girl with seizures, demonstrating the classical findings in complete lissencephaly. The cerebral cortex is remarkably thick and lacks gyri (smooth surface), the gray/white matter interface is smooth, and the sylvian fissures are underdeveloped. This appearance is very similar to the immature fetal brain.
sub_caption: Coronal T2WI of the brain of 1-year-old girl with seizures, demonstrating the classical findings in complete lissencephaly. The cerebral cortex is remarkably thick and lacks gyri (smooth surface), the gray/white matter interface is smooth, and the sylvian fissures are underdeveloped. This appearance is very similar to the immature fetal brain.
image_context: During the eighth fetal week the neurons start migrating from the germinal zone to the cortex. Several etiological factors have a role in interruption of neuronal migration, resulting in variable degrees of migrational abnormalities due to arrest of neurons at a distance short of the normal location of the cortex. In type 1 (classical) lissencephaly (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="asm-1-28f4">Figure 4</xref>), the brain surface is completely smooth (agyria) or has broad, flat gyri separated by a few, shallow sulci (pachygyria).), the brain surface is completely smooth (agyria) or has broad, flat gyri separated by a few, shallow sulci (pachygyria).1 In addition, imaging studies show a thick cortex and shallow, vertically oriented sylvian fissures (lack of opercularization) giving the cerebrum the shape of a figure of eight.17 More than one gene defect has been proved responsible for classical lissencephaly. In some types of classical lissencephaly, the mothers and sisters of the affected patients have another type of migrational defect (band heterotopia or double cortex).18 On the other hand, the previously termed type 2 lissencephaly is no longer considered lissencephaly, but a brain malformation that forms part of the cobblestone complex that is often seen in association with muscular dystrophy.1 Only one patient was identified in this series with this type of cerebral malformation. Gray matter heterotopia refers to normal neurons in abnormal location. Patients with heterotopia almost always present with epilepsy, the severity and prognosis of which is a function of the severity of heterotopia.17 The heterotopic gray matter may be located subependymally, subcortically, or between the ventricles and cortex. The heterotopic islands may have a nodular or band configuration and their signal intensity on MRI follows that of the cerebral cortex on all pulse sequences.19 These lesions are not surrounded by edema and do not enhance.

In some types of classical lissencephaly, the mothers and sisters of the affected patients have another type of migrational defect (band heterotopia or double cortex).18 On the other hand, the previously termed type 2 lissencephaly is no longer considered lissencephaly, but a brain malformation that forms part of the cobblestone complex that is often seen in association with muscular dystrophy.1 Only one patient was identified in this series with this type of cerebral malformation. Gray matter heterotopia refers to normal neurons in abnormal location. Patients with heterotopia almost always present with epilepsy, the severity and prognosis of which is a function of the severity of heterotopia.17 The heterotopic gray matter may be located subependymally, subcortically, or between the ventricles and cortex. The heterotopic islands may have a nodular or band configuration and their signal intensity on MRI follows that of the cerebral cortex on all pulse sequences.19 These lesions are not surrounded by edema and do not enhance.
